turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court in case 16-1435, Minnesota Voters Alliance v. Mansky. A Minnesota law prohibits individuals, including voters, from wearing a "political badge, political button or other political insignia" inside a polling place on Election Day. This political apparel ban applies only within the polling place itself and covers articles of clothing and accessories with political insignia upon them, a hat or a shirt with a logo on it, for example. The prohibition is in addition to a separate provision of Minnesota law banning campaign material. That is material showing support or opposition to a specific candidate, party, or ballot measure. State election judges, temporary government employees working the polls on Election Day, have the authority to decide whether a particular item falls within the political apparel ban. Violators are subject to a civil penalty or prosecution for a petty misdemeanor. A few days before the November 2010 election, petitioner Minnesota Voters Alliance and other plaintiffs brought a lawsuit challenging the political apparel ban as unconstitutional. The Federal District Court denied the plaintiffs' request to enjoin the law and on election day several voters associated with the plaintiffs ran into trouble with the ban. Petitioner Andrew Cilek, for example, was allegedly turned away from the polls for wearing a T-shirt with a Tea Party logo and the slogan "Don't Tread On Me" as well as a button displaying the words "Please I. D. Me". MVA and the other plaintiffs argued that the Minnesota law violated the First Amendment, but the District Court and the Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit rejected their argument and we granted review. Now, Minnesota's ban on political apparel plainly restricts a form of speech within the protection of the First Amendment, but that restriction applies only in a specific location, inside a polling place. It therefore calls for a kind of First Amendment review called Forum Analysis, which is used to assess speech restrictions applicable to government-controlled property. A polling place on Election Day qualifies as a so-called nonpublic forum under our cases. It is not like a public park or a town square, but is instead a highly regulated space that the government has set aside for a very specific purpose, casting a ballot in secret. The Government generally has more flexibility to impose speech restrictions in nonpublic forums. Such restrictions can even be based on the content of the speech, as Minnesota's law is here, so long as the law does not discriminate based on a speaker's viewpoint. The restriction must however be reasonable in light of the purpose served by the government forum. Now, applying that test, we agree with Minnesota that states may reasonably decide that some forms of advocacy should be excluded from the polling place in order to set it aside as an area apart from the clamor and din of electioneering. Casting a vote is a weighty civic act, akin to a jury's return of a verdict or a representative's vote on a piece of legislation. It is a time for choosing, not campaigning. States may reasonably decide that the interior of the polling place should reflect that distinction. And a majority of states do in fact agree with Minnesota that at least some kinds of campaign-related clothing and accessories should stay outside. That choice is worthy of respect from the courts. But the line the state draws must be reasonable. That means the state must be able to articulate a sensible basis for distinguishing what may come in from what must stay out and we find that Minnesota has not done that here. Again, the statute prohibits wearing a political badge, political button or other political insignia. The statute does not define the word "political", and Minnesota's understanding of that term is not limited to messages that can be fairly characterized as campaign speech, that is, items advertising one's support for or opposition to a particular candidate, party, or ballot question. Instead, Minnesota interprets the word to encompass a broader, less determinative class of items. For instance, the state's official guidance for election judges specifies that the ban covers issue-oriented material designed to impact or influence voting, but what qualifies as an issue? The answer, as far as we can tell from the state's briefing and argument, is any subject on which a political candidate or party has taken a stance or shared a view. For instance, a Support Our Troops shirt might be banned if one of the candidates or parties had expressed a view on military funding or aid to veterans. Such a rule would require an election judge to maintain a mental index of the platforms and positions of every candidate and party on the ballot. That is not reasonable. The state's official guidance also interprets the apparel ban to cover any item "promoting a group with recognizable political views", but the state does not limit that category to groups that have actually endorsed a candidate or taken a position on a ballot question. Instead the group need only have a view on the issues confronting voters in a given election. Now, any number of associations, educational institutions, businesses, religious organizations could be understand to have an opinion on a topic meeting that description. For instance, AARP, the World Wildlife Fund and Ben & Jerry's have stated positions on matters of public concern. Now, if the views of those groups align or conflict with the position of any candidate or party on the ballot, it seems that anything with their logos on could be banned. Now, the state emphasizes that the group's political positions need to be well-known, but we think that requirement only increases the potential for arbitrary enforcement, as it is likely to turn on the background knowledge and media consumption of the particular election judge screening voters at the door. Now, these open-ended standards for determining what qualifies as political pose riddles that even the state's top lawyers struggle to solve. For instance, the state represented oral argument that a shirt declaring 'All Lives Matter' would be banned because it could be perceived as political. A shirt displaying a rainbow flag on the other hand could be worn, unless there was an issue related to gay rights on the ballot. We were told that Minnesota law prohibits a voter from entering a polling place wearing a shirt displaying the text of the Second Amendment, but a shirt displaying the text of the First Amendment would be allowed. Now, the Constitution does not require all speech restrictions to be perfectly clear in all their applications. But the state's difficulty with the content of its law goes well beyond close calls on borderline cases and that is a serious matter when the whole point of the exercise is to prohibit the expression of political views. In the absence of objective workable standards an election judge's own politics may shape his views on what counts as political. For these reasons and others explained in our opinion, we hold that Minnesota has not supported its good intentions with a law capable of reasoned application and the First Amendment requires more. We reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit and remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Sotomayor has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justice Breyer has joined.
